# ============================================================

## INTRO COPY

Every week, Ohio businesses lose money to phishing — and most of them thought they'd never fall for it. Phishing emails look like real messages from real vendors. Phone calls sound like they're from your bank. Text messages say your account is locked. The thing that makes phishing so effective is that it doesn't exploit a tech gap — it exploits trust, urgency, and distraction.

This guide breaks down what phishing is, the different forms it takes, the red flags to watch for, and what concrete steps Northeast Ohio businesses can take to protect their teams and data.

---

## SECTION 1: What Phishing Actually Is

Phishing is a type of social engineering attack where someone tries to trick you into giving them sensitive information — passwords, account numbers, payment card data — by pretending to be a trusted person or company.

The attack usually arrives via email, but it can also come through phone calls (called vishing), text messages (smishing), or fake websites. The goal is almost always the same: get you to click a link, enter credentials, or send money before you have time to think.

Phishing works because it doesn't hack your systems — it hacks your judgment. That's why technical controls alone can't stop it. Your team needs to know what to look for.

---

## SECTION 2: The Most Common Types of Phishing

Phishing isn't a single technique — it's a family of tactics. Here are the forms Ohio businesses encounter most often:

**Email Phishing** — The classic. A message that looks like it's from Microsoft, Apple, Amazon, your bank, or a vendor you use. The sender address is slightly wrong (support@amaz0n.com instead of @amazon.com). The link goes to a fake login page.

**Spear Phishing** — Targeted at a specific person, not random. The attacker has done research: your name, your company's name, your role. These are much harder to spot because the email feels personal. Often used to target office managers, accountants, or executives.

**Whaling** — A spear phishing attack aimed specifically at a CEO, CFO, or other high-level decision-maker. The email might look like it's from a board member or a lawyer. The ask is usually a wire transfer or sensitive data.

**Vishing (Voice Phishing)** — A phone call from someone claiming to be from the IRS, your bank's fraud department, or tech support. They create urgency — arrest warrant, account being closed — to get you to act before you verify.

**Smishing (SMS Phishing)** — A text message with a link to track a package, verify a login, or claim a prize. Text messages have higher open rates than email and people trust them more.

**Clone Phishing** — An attacker copies a legitimate email you've received — an invoice, a Zoom link, a shared document — and sends it again from a fake address. Everything looks identical except the link.

---

## SECTION 3: Red Flags That Signal a Phishing Attempt

Here are the specific warning signs your team should be trained to notice:

**Urgent or threatening language** — 'Your account will be suspended', 'Immediate action required', 'Legal action pending'. Attackers want you to panic and act fast.

**Requests for sensitive information** — Your bank doesn't text you asking for your password. Microsoft doesn't call asking for your credit card. No legitimate vendor asks for login credentials over email or phone.

**Mismatched or suspicious URLs** — Hover over any link before clicking. Does the web address look right? Look for extra letters, numbers, or wrong domains (microsft.com vs microsoft.com).

**Unexpected attachments** — A shipment notification you didn't order, an invoice from a company you've never used, a 'receipt' for something you didn't buy. Never open an attachment you're not expecting.

**Sender address that doesn't match** — Look carefully. Attackers register domains that look almost right: paypa1.com (with a lowercase L), amazon-security.net, support@yourbank-online.com.

**Requests to send money via gift cards or wire transfer** — Any legitimate business asking for payment via gift cards is a scam. Always verify by calling the company directly using a number you know is real, not one they give you in the message.

**Generic greetings** — 'Dear Customer' or 'Dear User' instead of your actual name. Legitimate companies you have accounts with will usually address you by name.

**Spelling and grammar errors** — Professional companies don't send emails with obvious typos. Small errors are a real sign of a phishing attempt.

---

## SECTION 4: Real Phishing Scenarios That Hit Northeast Ohio Businesses

These are anonymized examples based on patterns NHM LLC has seen in the Canton, Akron, Cleveland, and Youngstown markets:

**The Fake Invoice** — An accounting clerk receives an email that looks like it's from a vendor they work with regularly, with an updated bank account for the next payment. The email is professional, the invoice looks real, and the amount is significant. The payment goes out and the money is gone.

**The Microsoft Login Page** — An employee gets an email saying their Office 365 password is expiring and they need to re-enter their credentials. The link goes to a page that looks exactly like Microsoft's login. They type in their email and password. The attacker now has access to their entire Microsoft account.

**The IRS Phone Call** — A business owner gets a call saying they owe back taxes and will be arrested if they don't pay immediately — usually via gift cards or wire transfer. The phone number looks local. The threat feels real. This is a scam. The IRS never demands payment over the phone or threatens arrest over the phone.

**The Amazon Text** — A text arrives saying a suspicious order was placed on your account and you should click a link to cancel it. The link goes to a fake Amazon login page that captures your credentials.

**The CEO Request** — An employee in accounts payable gets an email — supposedly from the CEO — asking them to urgently process a wire transfer. The email address is one letter off from the real CEO's address. The request is specific, time-sensitive, and seems legitimate.

---

## SECTION 5: How to Check If Your Email Is Being Spoofed

Business owners should check whether their own domain is being used in phishing campaigns against their employees or customers.

NHM LLC's free DomainGuard scan checks your domain's SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records and can tell you if your email is being spoofed. This is one of the most useful hygiene checks a small business can run — and it's free.

Run a free scan at /domain-checkup/ — it takes under a minute and you'll see immediately whether your domain is properly authenticated or leaving your employees and customers exposed to impersonation attacks.

---

## SECTION 6: How NHM Ohio Stops Phishing Before It Reaches Your Team

NHM LLC runs layered phishing protection across multiple stages:

**Email Authentication (SPF, DKIM, DMARC)** — We set up and monitor your email authentication records so attackers can't send emails from your domain and can't easily impersonate your domain in phishing attacks targeting your customers and vendors.

**Email Filtering and Sandboxing** — Link protection scans every URL in every email before your team clicks. Attachments are sandboxed and executed in a safe environment to detect malicious code before it reaches anyone.

**Endpoint Protection** — Even if something gets through, our monitored endpoint software catches it, isolates the device, and alerts our team within minutes. We watch for the behaviors that indicate a compromised account — impossible travel, multiple failed MFA attempts, unusual data access — and act before the attacker establishes persistence.

**Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** — Even if credentials are compromised, MFA stops the attacker from accessing your account. We roll out MFA across Microsoft 365, your VPN, and any other critical tool. Hardware keys, authenticator apps, and phone-based MFA each have different tradeoffs — we pick what's right for your team's workflow.

**Security Awareness Training** — We run simulated phishing tests so your team learns to recognize the real thing before it causes damage. We track results over time so you can see measurable improvement across your organization.

**24/7 Monitoring** — Our team watches your environment around the clock. If an account is showing signs of compromise, we catch it and act before the attacker gets further.

These controls work together. Email authentication stops spoofing, filtering stops malicious payloads, MFA stops credential misuse, and monitoring catches anything that slips through.

---

## SECTION 7: What to Do If You Think You've Been Phished

Time matters. The faster you act, the less damage the attacker can do. Don't panic — but move fast. Here's the order of operations:

1. **Change the compromised password immediately.** If you use that password anywhere else — and many people do — change it there too.

2. **Contact NHM LLC.** Our team can isolate compromised accounts, revoke active sessions, check for ongoing attacker access, and guide the next steps. Call (330) 587-9583.

3. **Notify your team.** If one person was phished, others may receive the same message. Tell them to ignore and delete it.

4. **Check your email rules and forwarding settings.** Attackers often set up automatic forwarding rules so they keep receiving copies of your emails even after you change your password.

5. **Check your Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace audit logs.** Look for unusual logins, app permissions, or mailbox rules created without your knowledge. These can indicate an attacker who has been sitting inside your environment for days or weeks.

6. **If money was transferred, contact your bank immediately.** The faster they know, the better chance they have of stopping or recovering the transfer.

7. **Document what happened** — screenshot the email, note the time, record what the attacker may have accessed. This matters for insurance claims and compliance reporting.

8. **Run a full security review.** If credentials were compromised, assume other access may have been established and do a thorough check. An attacker who gets in quietly often creates backdoor access they can use even after the initial password is changed.

---

## SECTION 8: Frequently Asked Questions

**Q: How do I know if an email from Microsoft or Apple is real?**
A: Hover over the sender's address. Real emails from Microsoft will come from @microsoft.com. Check the full domain carefully — attackers often use microsoftsupport.com or microsoft-365-login.com. When in doubt, open a new browser tab and go directly to the service's login page rather than clicking a link in the email.

**Q: Can phishing texts come from my own phone number?**
A: Sometimes. Caller ID can be spoofed. Don't trust a text just because it appears to come from your bank or a known number. Call the company directly using a number you find on their official website — not the number in the text message.

**Q: What is spear phishing vs regular phishing?**
A: Regular phishing is sent to many people hoping someone falls for it. Spear phishing is targeted — the attacker has researched a specific person and sends them a message designed specifically for them. Spear phishing is much harder to detect because the email feels personal and the sender may know real details about your life or business.

**Q: Does my business insurance cover phishing attacks?**
A: Many cyber insurance policies do cover phishing-related losses including fraudulent wire transfers and data recovery costs. Check with your insurer. NHM LLC can help you document what happened to support your claim.

**Q: How often should we train our team on phishing?**
A: At minimum, run simulated phishing tests quarterly. New team members should get training on day one. Review any real phishing attempts that did make it through and use them as immediate teaching moments — nothing is more memorable than catching a real attack in your own inbox.
